title: How Marty Supreme Put Oscar-Nominated Production Designer Jack Fisk in a New York State of Mind
text: Oscar-nominated production designer Jack Fisk took on a new challenge with director Josh Safdie's Marty Supreme, a 1952-set film starring Timothée Chalamet as an ambitious ping pong player navigating New York's Lower East Side. Initially hesitant about designing a film centered on table tennis, Fisk was drawn to Safdie's vision and enthusiasm. Importantly, the Lower East Side setting appealed to him: I hadn't really worked in New York my whole career, making this project particularly exciting. Fisk and Safdie exchanged archival photographs and design sketches, drawing on their personal connections to the neighborhood. They visited the Tenement Museum and discovered Ken Jacobs' 1955 short film Orchid Street, which documented street life and storefronts that proved invaluable to their design approach. The production designer developed modular storefront systems to replace modern glass facades with period-appropriate 1950s aesthetics. He worked with set decorator Adam Willis to create authentic awnings and crafted hundreds of hand-painted signs in Yiddish and English. The effort earned Fisk his fourth Oscar nomination, with the film receiving nine total nominations including Best Picture.
